preFix.title: Cloth corners on a quarter or half binding
preFix.subtitle: Cutting and applying the reinforced cloth triangles that protect the corners of a case-bound book
preFix.excerpt: The corners of a bound book take more wear than the rest of the cover. Cloth triangles mitre over the board corners and bond under the paper covering, protecting the most vulnerable points of a case binding.
preFix.sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapters on quarter and half bindings, including corner construction. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. Cockerell's diagrams show the mitre angle and turn-in sequence still followed today.

content.text: In a
half binding
the corners take the same cloth as the spine. In a
quarter binding
they stay plain, but cloth corners can be added as a reinforcement regardless of binding style. The technique is the same in either case: cut a cloth triangle, apply it to the board corner,
mitre
the tip, and fold the
turn-ins
over cleanly. Done well, the corner shows the grain of the cloth across the face of the board; done carelessly, it shows a lumpy fold.
What you need

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt blades. Change the craft-knife blade at the first sign of dulling. Cut on a self-healing mat with a metal ruler held firmly; keep the non-cutting hand at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path.
attrs.heading: Safety: cutting tools

content.text: Cutting the cloth triangles

Cut one cloth rectangle per corner. For a 2 mm board, the rectangle should be 50 mm square. This gives 10 mm of cloth visible on the board face and 10 mm of turn-in on each side, with a 30 mm mitre allowance at the tip.
Mark the cloth wrong-side-up with a pencil. Cut four 50 mm squares, one per corner.
On each square, mark a line 8 mm from the corner tip, parallel to the diagonal. This is the mitre cut line.
Do not cut along this line yet. Paste the cloth first, then trim.
Applying and mitring
Apply a thin, even coat of acid-free PVA to the wrong side of one cloth square with a small brush or finger. Work from the centre outward.
Place the board corner centred on the cloth so the board corner sits exactly at the diagonal mid-point of the square. Press down firmly.
Trim the cloth tip along the mitre cut line with the craft knife and ruler. Remove the triangle of waste cloth. This prevents a bulge at the corner tip when the turn-ins fold over.

Fold one turn-in flap over the board edge and rub it down firmly with the bone folder. Then fold the adjacent flap, pulling it gently taut before pressing it down. The two turn-ins meet at the corner tip and should align without overlapping.
Run the bone folder along the board edge in both directions to seat the cloth turn-in against the board. The edge should be straight and the cloth face unrucked.
Place the board under a weight for 30 minutes before applying the paper covering.
Common mistakes

items.fix: Trim the cloth tip to within 1 to 2 board-thicknesses of the corner before folding the turn-ins.
items.cause: The mitre cut was not made or the mitre left too much cloth.
items.symptom: The corner tip has a visible bump

items.fix: Trim one flap back with scissors so the two edges meet without overlapping.
items.cause: The mitre cut line was too far from the corner.
items.symptom: The two turn-in flaps overlap at the corner tip

items.fix: Dampen the surface slightly with a damp sponge to relax the PVA, reposition, and burnish with the bone folder working from the centre of the cloth outward.
items.cause: The cloth was pasted too wetly or placed unevenly.
items.symptom: Cloth wrinkles appear on the board face near the corner
